coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent turn-on nanosensor for selective detection of L-morphine using D-cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadian, Paria
Masteri-Farahani, Majid
Mosleh, Nazanin
coredata.dc:description: Here, L- and D-cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (L-/D-cys-GQDs) were designed with the aim of obtaining a selective fluorescent nanosensor to detect L-morphine. Citric acid was pyrolyzed to synthesize the GQDs, which were then functionalized with chiral L- and D-cys species using a thiol-ene click reaction between sulfur group of cysteine species and CC double bonds of GQDs. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) provides elemental analysis data which approved the presence of sulfur and nitrogen elements of L- and D-cysteine species on the surface of GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the particle size of the modified GQDs ranges from 2 to 4.2 nm. The results of fluorescence spectroscopy showed that upon functionalization of GQDs with L-/D-cys the fluorescence intensity decreases as a result of Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Interestingly, in the presence of L-morphine, the fluorescence intensity of D-cys-GQDs was selectively turned on as the FRET mechanism is ceased between the cysteine species and GQDs. Additional tests demonstrated that this nanosensor cannot interact with other drugs like methamphetamine or ibuprofen. As a result, it can serve as a cheap and precise nanosensor for identifying low quantities of L-morphine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
Graphene quantum dots
Morphine
Methamphetamine
Selective nanosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of chiral cysteine functionalized GQDs
Fluorescence studies of L-/D-cys-GQDs in the presence of opioids

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Kharazmi University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) EDX spectrum (inset: elemental percentage) and (b) TEM image of D-cys-GQDs (inset: size distribution); (c) UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of GQDs and L-/D-cys-GQDs.
(a) Full XPS and (b–e) expanded spectra of D-cys-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of D-cys-GQDs aqueous solution with L-MOR (inset: Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence enhancement of D-cys-GQDs). (b) Fluorescence spectra of L-cys-GQDs aqueous solution with L-MOR (inset: Corresponding Stern-Volmer plot). (c) Fluorescence spectra of D-cys-GQDs aqueous solution with D-methamphetamine (inset: Corresponding Stern-Volmer plot). (d) Fluorescence spectra of D-cys-GQDs aqueous solution with ibuprofen (inset: Corresponding Stern-Volmer plot).
Schematic mechanism of D-cys-GQDs as optical nanosensor of L-MOR.
Analytical characteristics for various techniques used to detect MOR.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent turn-on nanosensor for selective detection of L-morphine using D-cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran 15719-14911, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran 15719-14911, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Rochester Institute of Technology, Rochester, NY 14623, United State
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Rochester Institute of Technology, Rochester, NY 14623, United State

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, L- and D-cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (L-/D-cys-GQDs) were designed with the aim of obtaining a selective fluorescent nanosensor to detect L-morphine. Citric acid was pyrolyzed to synthesize the GQDs, which were then functionalized with chiral L- and D-cys species using a thiol-ene click reaction between sulfur group of cysteine species and CC double bonds of GQDs. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) provides elemental analysis data which approved the presence of sulfur and nitrogen elements of L- and D-cysteine species on the surface of GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the particle size of the modified GQDs ranges from 2 to 4.2 nm. The results of fluorescence spectroscopy showed that upon functionalization of GQDs with L-/D-cys the fluorescence intensity decreases as a result of Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Interestingly, in the presence of L-morphine, the fluorescence intensity of D-cys-GQDs was selectively turned on as the FRET mechanism is ceased between the cysteine species and GQDs. Additional tests demonstrated that this nanosensor cannot interact with other drugs like methamphetamine or ibuprofen. As a result, it can serve as a cheap and precise nanosensor for identifying low quantities of L-morphine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the development of selective sensor probes has attracted a great deal of academic research attention due to their potential use in various domains, like analytical chemistry and environmental applications [1,2]. As a type of selective nanosensor, fluorescent chiral nanostructures containing chiral selector species can identify and distinguish various kinds of enantiomers. The development of synthesis methods and processes has led to the investigation of chiral nanostructures using a variety of materials, like chiral organic compounds, metallic nanoparticles, and quantum dots, which have a wide range of possible useful applications [3–5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of carbon nanomaterial with a size of 10 nm or less which has a high fluorescence quantum yield, adjustable fluorescence, and low toxicity [6–9]. Due to their outstanding qualities, including good solubility and enhanced optical characteristics owing to the quantum confinement, GQDs have recently received a lot of research interest [10–14]. GQDs are one of the newest and most propitious nanostructures for future use in biosensors, bio-imaging, and chemical sensors due to their unique features [15–18].

The edge of GQDs layers can be substantially functionalized by organic molecules for energy conversion and sensing applications [19,20]. Functionalizing agents are critical for improving the efficacy of GQD-based biosensors by increasing signal transduction, selectivity, and sensitivity. Their ability to specifically bind to target molecules enables more precise and dependable detection, even at extremely low concentrations [21–24]. Functionalizing agents also improve biocompatibility by attaching biocompatible molecules, reducing potential toxicity and increasing stability in biological environments [25]. Furthermore, they enhance the stability and durability of sensors by protecting GQDs from environmental factors such as pH fluctuations and ionic strength variations. In this context, cysteine, which is a chiral amino acid containing S-H groups, has the potential to be employed as a tool for modifying GQDs. In addition, the NH2 and COOH groups in this molecule can provide good solubility and biocompatibility [26]. Therefore, functionalization of the surface of GQDs surface with chiral cysteine species not only improves the surface active sites but also amplifies their solubility. Consequently, the functionalized GQDs with cysteine species can be utilized for the discernment of chiral drugs.

Morphine (MOR) is a natural opioid with significant analgesic and sedative properties [27,28]. The precise detection of MOR holds great significance and has been widely adopted by numerous organizations. Many techniques have been published up to this point for the detection of MOR, but it is still difficult to come up with quick, easy, selective, sensitive, and practical methods for MOR detection [29].

Due to the significance of MOR detection, several nanosensors based on quantum dots have been developed [30–33]. To continue our ongoing research [31], we present a cost-effective and straightforward sensing technique that is based on thiol-ene click chemistry to obtain L-/D-cys-GQDs as a selective nanosensor for MOR detection. “Click” is a word that was first used by K. B. Sharpless in 2001 to characterize the reactions that are quick, easy to carry out, regiospecific, and offer high product yields [34,35]. The thiol-ene reaction is one of the most significant click reactions, which includes the addition of thiol (SH) group with an alkene (CC bond). It could be used for surface coatings, thin films, and sensing because of its many advantages, including environmental friendliness, a fast reaction rate, and a high yield [36–41].

After the design and preparation of the desired nanosensor, its application for MOR detection is investigated. This sensing strategy has the potential to introduce new opportunities for developing effective nanosensors for detecting and measuring various chiral analytes.
The details of the utilized chemicals and instruments are given in the supporting information.

GQDs were produced using citric acid as a precursor in a direct pyrolysis procedure [42]. 2 g of citric acid was added into a 5 mL beaker and subjected to heating until it reached a temperature of 200 °C. After 5 min, the citric acid melted and the color changed to yellow. After 20 min of heating, a light orange color showed up, signifying the creation of GQDs. It is crucial to reach this temperature since producing GQDs at temperatures lower than 200 °C is not possible. Furthermore, excessive heating time was avoided since it might lead to the production of graphene oxide. The obtained orange liquid containing GQDs was then slowly poured, drop by drop, into 100 mL of a 10 g·L−1 sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution while being stirred. The GQDs were produced in the aqueous solution after being neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH. The yellow GQDs solution was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to prepare the yellow GQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chiral cysteine functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs (0.4 g) were added to a round-bottom flask containing dimethylformamide (DMF, 10 mL) while it was being stirred continuously for 30 min under nitrogen gas. Separately, L-cysteine (0.096 g, 0.8 mmol) was dissolved in DMF (5 mL). After mixing the above-prepared solutions for 30 min, azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) (0.131 g, 0.8 mmol) was added as an initiator. The prepared mixture was heated for 2 h at 80 °C in an oil-bath. Then, heating was stopped, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 18 h at room temperature. Following the centrifugation process, the separated solid product underwent repeated washing with ethanol in order to eliminate any contaminants present. Subsequently, it was subjected to an overnight vacuum drying process to achieve L-cys-GQDs. Following a similar procedure, D-cys-GQDs were obtained by substituting L-cysteine for D-cysteine.

Aqueous solutions of L-MOR sulfate (1.32 mM), D-methamphetamine hydrochloride (5.07 mM), and ibuprofen (9.6 mM) were prepared at room temperature. To conduct the photoluminescence experiments, 0–200 μL of L-MOR sulfate solution was added to 3 mL of L-/D-cys-GQDs (2 mg·mL−1) in deionized water (DI). The fluorescence spectra of the combined solutions were then recorded with excitation wavelengths of 340 nm. Additionally, under the same circumstances, the selectivity of L-/D-cys-GQDs towards other drugs, such as ibuprofen and D-methamphetamine hydrochloride, was compared with that of L-MOR sulfate.

The stepwise production of L-/D-cys-GQDs is schematically shown in Scheme 1 , which includes the synthesis of GQDs with utilizing citric acid as a precursor and the functionalization of GQDs with chiral L-/D-cys via the thiol-ene click reaction in the presence of AIBN as an initiator.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to evaluate the elemental composition of D-cys-GQDs (Fig. 1 a). The existence of C, O, N, and S was confirmed by the EDX spectrum, which showed that the functionalization of GQDs with cysteine species was successful. Moreover, CHN analysis was conducted to further approve and determine the extent of functionalization with cysteine species. According to the CHN analysis, the amount of functionalization with L- and D-cysteine species was found to be 2.1 and 1.8 mmol·g−1, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to analyze the size of the obtained D-cys-GQDs particles and determine their particle size distribution (Fig. 1b). The particle size distribution of the D-cys-GQDs depicted in the inset of Fig. 2 b showed that their mean particle size is about 3.0 nm, while the particle size ranges from 2 to 4.2 nm. Moreover, the TEM image also showed the relatively uniform size and morphology of the D-cys-GQDs.

The optical characteristics of the prepared L-/D-cys-GQDs were examined utilizing UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of pure GQDs and L-/D-cys-GQDs (Fig. 1c) showed a peak at about 357 nm that correlated to the electronic transitions from n → π* assigned to the functional groups, including CO groups. The emission peaks of GQDs and L-/D-cys-GQDs in fluorescence spectra are located at about 440 nm, which showed a Stokes shift of 100 nm with regard to the used excitation wavelengths of 340 nm (Fig. 1c). The quenching effect of L-/D-cys-GQDs should be noticed following the functionalization step, which caused a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs.

The elemental composition of the produced D-cys-GQDs was revealed by a detailed analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The presence of C, O, N, and S as the primary building block elements was demonstrated by the D-cys-GQDs sample survey XPS scan (Fig. 2a). A deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectra of D-cys-GQDs (Fig. 2b) yields three components: CC/CC, CO, and CO from 284 to 289 eV. Two peaks from 532 to 534 eV in the spectra of O1s, shown in Fig. 2c, are identified as the CO and COH bands. Fig. 2d depicts the N1s spectrum, which has two peaks at 400.28 eV (free NH2) and 401.98 eV (hydrogen bonded NH2). It was confirmed that the thiol group was successfully attached to the surface of the GQDs by the observation of free NH2 in the N1s peak deconvolution analysis. Two peaks in the area of 163–165 are recognized as S2p 3/2 and S2p 1/2 and attributed to the sulfur group of attached D-cysteine species in the high-resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 2e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence studies of L-/D-cys-GQDs in the presence of opioids
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence spectra of L-/D-cys-GQDs were studied in the presence of various opioids, including L-MOR, D-methamphetamine, and ibuprofen, in order to assess their fluorescence behavior (Fig. 3 ). By varying the concentrations of L-MOR from 0 to 200 μL, the fluorescence changes of D-cys-GQDs were evaluated to investigate the L-MOR detection sensitivity. Fig. 3a demonstrates that as L-MOR concentrations increased, the fluorescence intensities steadily increased. The following Stern-Volmer equation provides a satisfactory description of how effectively D-cys-GQDs increase fluorescence intensity when combined with L-MOR:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
∙
[ Q ]

In this equation, KSV stands for the Stern-Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of L-MOR, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of D-cys-GQDs in the absence and presence of L-MOR, respectively. The Stern-Volmer plot, on the other hand, is inversely connected to the plot of F/F0 versus the concentration of L-MOR, and this plot results in a similar linear plot that suggests the dynamic mechanism is effectively governing the interaction of L-/D-cys-GQDs with L-MOR or other opioids. It is interesting to note that, as represented in the inset of Fig. 3a, the slope of F/F0 versus L-MOR concentration is linear, showing that L-MOR interacts with the excited state of D-cys-GQDs as opposed to the ground state. Additionally, an accurate assessment of the L-MOR concentration in samples is possible thanks to the strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.9947). However, with the addition of L-MOR to L-cys-GQDs, there were hardly any changes in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3b). As is evident, the obtained K value resulted in the plot of F/F0 versus L-MOR being ≈ 0. To comprehend how D-cys-GQDs interact with various concentrations of those narcotics, the impact of adding various quantities of D-methamphetamine and ibuprofen on the fluorescence spectrum of D-cys-GQDs was investigated (Fig. 3c and d). The variations in the fluorescence intensities of D-cys-GQDs caused by increasing the concentration of D-methamphetamine and ibuprofen are insignificant, in contrast to the obvious changes in the case of L-MOR with D-cys-GQDs. As a result, it was discovered that L-MOR had a different impact on the fluorescence of D-cys-GQDs than other narcotics, including D-methamphetamine and ibuprofen. The discrimination behavior of L-/D-cys-GQDs could be attributed to the chiral cysteine species that are presented on their exterior surfaces, which cause them to differently interact with chiral compounds like L-MOR and D-methamphetamine. It is conceivable to create a sensitive and selective nanosensor for L-MOR detection by taking this effect into account. Moreover, to provide a comprehensive understanding and detailed visual representation of the fluorescence changes induced by the addition of L-morphine, we prepared a photographic documentation, as shown in Fig. S1 in which the fluorescent intensity of D-cys-GQDs under UV light increased progressively with the addition of L-MOR.

On the other hand, the test was reproduced three times by adding L-MOR to the D-cys-GQDs sensor and the fluorescence spectra were analyzed. The correlated Stern-Volmer plots indicated slight changes in the spectra, demonstrating the stability and reproducibility of the sensor under these conditions (Fig. S2).

Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) serves as the basis for the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of D-cys-GQDs in the presence of L-MOR. As shown in Scheme 2 , FRET provides a mechanism for quenching the fluorescence of GQDs through the energy transfer from GQDs to attached D-cysteine species. The addition of L-MOR blocks the FRET from GQDs to D-cysteine species, which results in turning on the fluorescence of D-cys-GQDs. The other narcotic substances have little to no impact on the fluorescence intensity of D-cys-GQDs as they cannot fit into the receptor sites. Due to its ability to distinguish between low concentrations of L-MOR and other species like D-methamphetamine and ibuprofen, this mechanism has enabled the selective detection of L-MOR.

It was determined that the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.99 µM. Table 1 compares the detection limit of this approach with other documented techniques for a few comparable substances. When compared to other complex systems that require costly instruments, it is evident that this easily prepared nanosensor has good sensitivity. As a result, the produced D-cys-GQDs may be applied as a selective nanosensor for measuring traces of L-MOR.

In summary, we have shown that L-/D-cys-GQDs can be produced using a straightforward procedure that involves the pyrolysis of citric acid to achieve GQDs and the thiol-ene click reaction to functionalize GQDs with L-/D-cys. Additionally, the fluorescence behavior of the as-prepared L-/D-cys-GQDs was studied in the presence of several narcotics, such as L-MOR, D-methamphetamine, and ibuprofen. Our findings revealed that the fluorescence intensity of D-cys-GQDs was selectively activated by L-MOR, with no significant interaction observed with other tested drugs such as ibuprofen or D-methamphetamine. This remarkable selectivity suggests a specific interaction mechanism between D-cys-GQDs and L-MOR, potentially rooted in the stereochemical configuration and binding affinities of the molecules involved. Furthermore, the sensitivity of D-cys-GQDs to L-MOR was quantified, showing a detectable fluorescence response at even low concentrations, underscoring their potential application in trace detection. This high sensitivity combined with selective activation indicates that D-cys-GQDs could serve as a powerful tool for the rapid and accurate detection of L-MOR in complex biological samples. The synthesis process is not only straightforward but also scalable, making it feasible for widespread application. This research opens new avenues for the development of GQD-based nanosensors with high selectivity and sensitivity for specific targets. The intriguing nature of our findings lies in the unique interaction between D-cys-GQDs and L-MOR, paving the way for future studies to explore the underlying mechanisms and expand the application of GQDs in the field of drug detection and beyond.

Paria Mohammadian: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Majid Masteri-Farahani: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Nazanin Mosleh: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Kharazmi University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115970.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
